A space volcano? 
These two interacting galaxies almost look like an erupting volcano. Called MCG+12-02-001, this pair is visibly affected by their gravitational interaction, as cosmic material flings out in opposite directions.
Located about 200 million light-years away, these galaxies call the constellation Cassiopeia home: https://go.nasa.gov/3QsN5K6
Image credit: NASA, ESA, the Hubble Heritage (STScI/AURA)-ESA/Hubble Collaboration, and A. Evans (University of Virginia, Charlottesville/NRAO/Stony Brook University)

xtijo5x x1o0tod x10l6tqk x13vifvy xh8yej3 xl1xv1r" alt="Composite image of edge-on spiral starburst galaxy Messier 82 as observed by NASA's James Webb and Hubble space telescopes. Hourglass-shaped plumes of gas are shooting outward from above and below a bright blue-white, disk-shaped center. The plumes are yellow near the galaxy’s bright center, denoting areas of ionized hydrogen gas as observed by Hubble, and gradually become redder as you move farther away. Messier 82 is set against the black background of space, which has many distant galaxies that appear as small white and orange spirals, ovals, and points of light. Toward the right of Messier 82 is a blue-white star with eight-pointed diffraction spikes that are characteristic of Webb." referrerpolicy="origin-when-cross-origin" xyri2b x18d9i69 x1c1uobl x1hl2dhg x16tdsg8 x1vvkbs x3nfvp2 x1j61x8r x1fcty0u xdj266r xat24cr xm2jcoa x1mpyi22 xxymvpz xlup9mm x1kky2od"><img height="16" width="16" class="xz74otr x15mokao x1ga7v0g x16uus16 xbiv7yw" alt="🎶" referrerpolicy="origin-when-cross-origin" src="https://static.xx.fbcdn.net/images/emoji.php/v9/t1f/1/16/1f3b6.png"></span>

Webb’s new view of M82, added to archival data from NASA's Hubble Space Telescope, is giving us a more complete picture of this starburst galaxy. Because Webb can see infrared light, it is able to peer through clouds of dust and gas to see the shape of this edge-on galaxy, as well as approximately 16.5 million of its stars. M82’s rapid star formation, thought to be the result of its merger with another galaxy, will only be a (relatively) brief period in its history. Ironically, the extreme star formation is causing plumes of material to be ejected above and below the disk of the galaxy - something that will disrupt future stellar birth.

Many of the features of this galaxy were blocked dust and gas at visible wavelengths of light. Webb is capturing never-before-seen detail, including the galaxy’s uniquely distorted disk, likely a result of the galaxy merger - and a staggering number of stars. These 16.5 million are likely only a small portion of the total number of stars that might reside in a galaxy like M82. Hubble data, in yellow, show tendrils of material, which represent ionized hydrogen gas, close to the galaxy’s disk. 

Galaxies are intricate ecosystems, and telescopes working together are helping to unlock M82’s mysteries.

Read more at the link in the comments.

by the pocketful \ud83c\udfb6\n\nWebb\u2019s new view of M82, added to archival data from NASA's Hubble Space Telescope, is giving us a more complete picture of this starburst galaxy. Because Webb can see infrared light, it is able to peer through clouds of dust and gas to see the shape of this edge-on galaxy, as well as approximately 16.5 million of its stars. M82\u2019s rapid star formation, thought to be the result of its merger with another galaxy, will only be a (relatively) brief period in its history. Ironically, the extreme star formation is causing plumes of material to be ejected above and below the disk of the galaxy - something that will disrupt future stellar birth.\n\nMany of the features of this galaxy were blocked dust and gas at visible wavelengths of light. Webb is capturing never-before-seen detail, including the galaxy\u2019s uniquely distorted disk, likely a result of the galaxy merger - and a staggering number of stars. These 16.5 million are likely only a small portion of the total number of stars that might reside in a galaxy like M82. Hubble data, in yellow, show tendrils of material, which represent ionized hydrogen gas, close to the galaxy\u2019s disk. \n\nGalaxies are intricate ecosystems, and telescopes working together are helping to unlock M82\u2019s mysteries.\n\nRead more at the link in the comments.  \n\nCredit: Image: NASA, ESA, CSA, Adam Smercina (STScI, Tufts), Thomas Williams (University of Manchester); Image Processing: Alyssa Pagan (STScI)"},"message_truncation_line_limit":2,"th_dat_spo":null,"attachments":[{"action_links":[]}],"id":"UzpfSTEwMDA2NDQ0NjQ0NDI4NzoxNDE4NTc4MTUzNjMzNzE5OjE0MTg1NzgxNTM2MzM3MTk="},"__module_operation_CometFeedStoryMessageMatchRenderer_story":{"__dr":"CometFeedStoryDefaultMessageRenderingStrategy_message$normalization.graphql"},"__module_component_CometFeedStoryMessageMatchRenderer_story":{"__dr":"CometFeedStoryDefaultMessageRenderingStrategy.react"}},"message_suffix":null,"message_container":{"__typename":"CometFeedStoryAutoTranslatedMessageContainerRenderingStrategy","__isICometStorySection":"CometFeedStoryAutoTranslatedMessageContainerRenderingStrategy","is_prod_eligible":true,"story":{"is_text_only_story":false,"translatability_for_viewer":{"source_dialect":"en_XX"},"post_level_music_info":{"audio_asset":null},"translation":{"message":{"delight_ranges":[],"image_ranges":[],"inline_style_ranges":[],"aggregated_ranges":[],"ranges":[],"color_ranges":[],"text":"Ng\u00f4i sao b\u00ean t\u00fai \ud83c\udfb6\n\nQuan \u0111i\u1ec3m m\u1edbi c\u1ee7a Webb v\u1ec1 M82, \u0111\u01b0\u1ee3c th\u00eam v\u00e0o d\u1eef li\u1ec7u l\u01b0u tr\u1eef t\u1eeb \u0040TAG, \u0111ang mang \u0111\u1ebfn cho ch\u00fang ta m\u1ed9t b\u1ee9c tranh ho\u00e0n ch\u1ec9nh h\u01a1n v\u1ec1 thi\u00ean h\u00e0 sao n\u00e0y. B\u1edfi v\u00ec Webb c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 nh\u00ecn th\u1ea5y \u00e1nh s\u00e1ng h\u1ed3ng ngo\u1ea1i, n\u00f3 c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 nh\u00ecn qua nh\u1eefng \u0111\u00e1m m\u00e2y b\u1ee5i v\u00e0 kh\u00ed \u0111\u1ec3 nh\u00ecn th\u1ea5y h\u00ecnh d\u1ea1ng c\u1ee7a thi\u00ean h\u00e0 c\u1ea1nh n\u00e0y, c\u0169ng nh\u01b0 kho\u1ea3ng 16,5 tri\u1ec7u ng\u00f4i sao c\u1ee7a n\u00f3. S\u1ef1 h\u00ecnh th\u00e0nh ng\u00f4i sao nhanh c\u1ee7a M82, \u0111\u01b0\u1ee3c cho l\u00e0 k\u1ebft qu\u1ea3 c\u1ee7a vi\u1ec7c s\u00e1p nh\u1eadp v\u1edbi m\u1ed9t d\u1ea3i ng\u00e2n h\u00e0 kh\u00e1c, s\u1ebd ch\u1ec9 l\u00e0 m\u1ed9t kho\u1ea3ng th\u1eddi gian ng\u1eafn (t\u01b0\u01a1ng \u0111\u1ed1i) trong l\u1ecbch s\u1eed c\u1ee7a n\u00f3. Tr\u1edb tr\u00eau thay, s\u1ef1 h\u00ecnh th\u00e0nh ng\u00f4i sao c\u1ef1c \u0111oan \u0111ang khi\u1ebfn c\u00e1c v\u1eadt li\u1ec7u b\u1ecb tr\u00fat ra tr\u00ean v\u00e0 d\u01b0\u1edbi \u0111\u0129a c\u1ee7a thi\u00ean h\u00e0 - th\u1ee9 g\u00ec \u0111\u00f3 s\u1ebd ph\u00e1 v\u1ee1 s\u1ef1 sinh ra sao trong t\u01b0\u01a1ng lai.\n\nNhi\u1ec1u t\u00ednh n\u0103ng c\u1ee7a thi\u00ean h\u00e0 n\u00e0y \u0111\u00e3 b\u1ecb ch\u1eb7n b\u1ee5i v\u00e0 kh\u00ed \u1edf b\u01b0\u1edbc s\u00f3ng \u00e1nh s\u00e1ng c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 nh\u00ecn th\u1ea5y. Webb \u0111ang ghi l\u1ea1i nh\u1eefng chi ti\u1ebft ch\u01b0a t\u1eebng th\u1ea5y, bao g\u1ed3m c\u1ea3 \u0111\u0129a b\u1ecb m\u00e9o \u0111\u1ed9c \u0111\u00e1o c\u1ee7a thi\u00ean h\u00e0, c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 l\u00e0 k\u1ebft qu\u1ea3 c\u1ee7a vi\u1ec7c h\u1ee3p nh\u1ea5t thi\u00ean h\u00e0 - v\u00e0 m\u1ed9t s\u1ed1 ng\u00f4i sao \u0111\u00e1ng kinh ng\u1ea1c. 16,5 tri\u1ec7u n\u00e0y c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 ch\u1ec9 l\u00e0 m\u1ed9t ph\u1ea7n nh\u1ecf trong t\u1ed5ng s\u1ed1 c\u00e1c ng\u00f4i sao c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 c\u01b0 tr\u00fa trong m\u1ed9t d\u1ea3i ng\u00e2n h\u00e0 nh\u01b0 M82. D\u1eef li\u1ec7u Hubble, m\u00e0u v\u00e0ng, cho th\u1ea5y c\u00e1c g\u00e2n c\u1ee7a v\u1eadt li\u1ec7u, \u0111\u1ea1i di\u1ec7n cho kh\u00ed hydrogen ion h\u00f3a, g\u1ea7n v\u1edbi \u0111\u0129a c\u1ee7a thi\u00ean h\u00e0.\n\nThi\u00ean h\u00e0 l\u00e0 h\u1ec7 sinh th\u00e1i ph\u1ee9c t\u1ea1p, v\u00e0 k\u00ednh thi\u00ean v\u0103n h\u1ee3p t\u00e1c c\u00f9ng nhau \u0111ang gi\u00fap khai m\u1edf nh\u1eefng b\u00ed \u1ea9n c\u1ee7a M82.\n\n\u0110\u1ecdc th\u00eam t\u1ea1i link trong ph\u1ea7n b\u00ecnh lu\u1eadn.\n\nNgu\u1ed3n: H\u00ecnh \u1ea3nh: NASA, ESA, CSA, Adam Smercina (STScI, by the pocketful \ud83c\udfb6\n\nWebb\u2019s new view of M82, added to archival data from NASA's Hubble Space Telescope, is giving us a more complete picture of this starburst galaxy. Because Webb can see infrared light, it is able to peer through clouds of dust and gas to see the shape of this edge-on galaxy, as well as approximately 16.5 million of its stars. M82\u2019s rapid star formation, thought to be the result of its merger with another galaxy, will only be a (relatively) brief period in its history. Ironically, the extreme star formation is causing plumes of material to be ejected above and below the disk of the galaxy - something that will disrupt future stellar birth.\n\nMany of the features of this galaxy were blocked dust and gas at visible wavelengths of light. Webb is capturing never-before-seen detail, including the galaxy\u2019s uniquely distorted disk, likely a result of the galaxy merger - and a staggering number of stars. These 16.5 million are likely only a small portion of the total number of stars that might reside in a galaxy like M82. Hubble data, in yellow, show tendrils of material, which represent ionized hydrogen gas, close to the galaxy\u2019s disk. \n\nGalaxies are intricate ecosystems, and telescopes working together are helping to unlock M82\u2019s mysteries.\n\nRead more at the link in the comments.  \n\nCredit: Image: NASA, ESA, CSA, Adam Smercina (STScI, Tufts), Thomas Williams (University of Manchester); Image by the pocketful \ud83c\udfb6\n\nWebb\u2019s new view of M82, added to archival data from NASA's Hubble Space Telescope, is giving us a more complete picture of this starburst galaxy. Because Webb can see infrared light, it is able to peer through clouds of dust and gas to see the shape of this edge-on galaxy, as well as approximately 16.5 million of its stars. M82\u2019s rapid star formation, thought to be the result of its merger with another galaxy, will only be a (relatively) brief period in its history. Ironically, the extreme star formation is causing plumes of material to be ejected above and below the disk of the galaxy - something that will disrupt future stellar birth.\n\nMany of the features of this galaxy were blocked dust and gas at visible wavelengths of light. Webb is capturing never-before-seen detail, including the galaxy\u2019s uniquely distorted disk, likely a result of the galaxy merger - and a staggering number of stars. These 16.5 million are likely only a small portion of the total number of stars that might reside in a galaxy like M82. Hubble data, in yellow, show tendrils of material, which represent ionized hydrogen gas, close to the galaxy\u2019s disk. \n\nGalaxies are intricate ecosystems, and telescopes working together are helping to unlock M82\u2019s mysteries.\n\nRead more at the link in the comments.  \n\nCredit: Image: NASA, ESA, CSA, Adam Smercina (STScI, Tufts), Thomas Williams (University of Manchester); Image Processing: Alyssa Pagan (STScI)"},"message_truncation_line_limit":2,"th_dat_spo":null,"attachments":[{"action_links":[]}],"id":"UzpfSTEwMDA2NDQ0NjQ0NDI4NzoxNDE4NTc4MTUzNjMzNzE5OjE0MTg1NzgxNTM2MzM3MTk="},"__module_operation_CometFeedStoryMessageMatchRenderer_story":{"__dr":"CometFeedStoryDefaultMessageRenderingStrategy_message$normalization.graphql"},"__module_component_CometFeedStoryMessageMatchRenderer_story":{"__dr":"CometFeedStoryDefaultMessageRenderingStrategy.react"}},"message_suffix":null,"message_container":{"__typename":"CometFeedStoryAutoTranslatedMessageContainerRenderingStrategy","__isICometStorySection":"CometFeedStoryAutoTranslatedMessageContainerRenderingStrategy","is_prod_eligible":true,"story":{"is_text_only_story":false,"translatability_for_viewer":{"source_dialect":"en_XX"},"post_level_music_info":{"audio_asset":null},"translation":{"message":{"delight_ranges":[],"image_ranges":[],"inline_style_ranges":[],"aggregated_ranges":[],"ranges":[],"color_ranges":[],"text":"Ng\u00f4i sao b\u00ean t\u00fai \ud83c\udfb6\n\nQuan \u0111i\u1ec3m m\u1edbi c\u1ee7a Webb v\u1ec1 M82, \u0111\u01b0\u1ee3c th\u00eam v\u00e0o d\u1eef li\u1ec7u l\u01b0u tr\u1eef t\u1eeb \u0040TAG, \u0111ang mang \u0111\u1ebfn cho ch\u00fang ta m\u1ed9t b\u1ee9c tranh ho\u00e0n ch\u1ec9nh h\u01a1n v\u1ec1 thi\u00ean h\u00e0 sao n\u00e0y. B\u1edfi v\u00ec Webb c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 nh\u00ecn th\u1ea5y \u00e1nh s\u00e1ng h\u1ed3ng ngo\u1ea1i, n\u00f3 c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 nh\u00ecn qua nh\u1eefng \u0111\u00e1m m\u00e2y b\u1ee5i v\u00e0 kh\u00ed \u0111\u1ec3 nh\u00ecn th\u1ea5y h\u00ecnh d\u1ea1ng c\u1ee7a thi\u00ean h\u00e0 c\u1ea1nh n\u00e0y, c\u0169ng nh\u01b0 kho\u1ea3ng 16,5 tri\u1ec7u ng\u00f4i sao c\u1ee7a n\u00f3. S\u1ef1 h\u00ecnh th\u00e0nh ng\u00f4i sao nhanh c\u1ee7a M82, \u0111\u01b0\u1ee3c cho l\u00e0 k\u1ebft qu\u1ea3 c\u1ee7a vi\u1ec7c s\u00e1p nh\u1eadp v\u1edbi m\u1ed9t d\u1ea3i ng\u00e2n h\u00e0 kh\u00e1c, s\u1ebd ch\u1ec9 l\u00e0 m\u1ed9t kho\u1ea3ng th\u1eddi gian ng\u1eafn (t\u01b0\u01a1ng \u0111\u1ed1i) trong l\u1ecbch s\u1eed c\u1ee7a n\u00f3. Tr\u1edb tr\u00eau thay, s\u1ef1 h\u00ecnh th\u00e0nh ng\u00f4i sao c\u1ef1c \u0111oan \u0111ang khi\u1ebfn c\u00e1c v\u1eadt li\u1ec7u b\u1ecb tr\u00fat ra tr\u00ean v\u00e0 d\u01b0\u1edbi \u0111\u0129a c\u1ee7a thi\u00ean h\u00e0 - th\u1ee9 g\u00ec \u0111\u00f3 s\u1ebd ph\u00e1 v\u1ee1 s\u1ef1 sinh ra sao trong t\u01b0\u01a1ng lai.\n\nNhi\u1ec1u t\u00ednh n\u0103ng c\u1ee7a thi\u00ean h\u00e0 n\u00e0y \u0111\u00e3 b\u1ecb ch\u1eb7n b\u1ee5i v\u00e0 kh\u00ed \u1edf b\u01b0\u1edbc s\u00f3ng \u00e1nh s\u00e1ng c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 nh\u00ecn th\u1ea5y. Webb \u0111ang ghi l\u1ea1i nh\u1eefng chi ti\u1ebft ch\u01b0a t\u1eebng th\u1ea5y, bao g\u1ed3m c\u1ea3 \u0111\u0129a b\u1ecb m\u00e9o \u0111\u1ed9c \u0111\u00e1o c\u1ee7a thi\u00ean h\u00e0, c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 l\u00e0 k\u1ebft qu\u1ea3 c\u1ee7a vi\u1ec7c h\u1ee3p nh\u1ea5t thi\u00ean h\u00e0 - v\u00e0 m\u1ed9t s\u1ed1 ng\u00f4i sao \u0111\u00e1ng kinh ng\u1ea1c. 16,5 tri\u1ec7u n\u00e0y c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 ch\u1ec9 l\u00e0 m\u1ed9t ph\u1ea7n nh\u1ecf trong t\u1ed5ng s\u1ed1 c\u00e1c ng\u00f4i sao c\u00f3 th\u1ec3 c\u01b0 tr\u00fa trong m\u1ed9t d\u1ea3i ng\u00e2n h\u00e0 nh\u01b0 M82. D\u1eef li\u1ec7u Hubble, m\u00e0u v\u00e0ng, cho th\u1ea5y c\u00e1c g\u00e2n c\u1ee7a v\u1eadt li\u1ec7u, \u0111\u1ea1i di\u1ec7n cho kh\u00ed hydrogen ion h\u00f3a, g\u1ea7n v\u1edbi \u0111\u0129a c\u1ee7a thi\u00ean h\u00e0.\n\nThi\u00ean h\u00e0 l\u00e0 h\u1ec7 sinh th\u00e1i ph\u1ee9c t\u1ea1p, v\u00e0 k\u00ednh thi\u00ean v\u0103n h\u1ee3p t\u00e1c c\u00f9ng nhau \u0111ang gi\u00fap khai m\u1edf nh\u1eefng b\u00ed \u1ea9n c\u1ee7a M82.\n\n\u0110\u1ecdc th\u00eam t\u1ea1i link trong ph\u1ea7n b\u00ecnh lu\u1eadn.\n\nNgu\u1ed3n: H\u00ecnh \u1ea3nh: NASA, ESA, CSA, Adam Smercina (STScI, Hubble data, in yellow, show tendrils of material, which represent ionized hydrogen gas, close to the galaxy\u2019s disk. \n\nGalaxies are intricate ecosystems, and telescopes working together are by the pocketful \ud83c\udfb6\n\nWebb\u2019s new view of M82, added to archival data from NASA's Hubble Space Telescope, is giving us a more complete picture of this starburst galaxy. Because Webb can see infrared light, it is able to peer through clouds of dust and gas to see the shape of this edge-on galaxy, as well as approximately 16.5 million of its stars. M82\u2019s rapid star formation, thought to be the result of its merger with another galaxy, will only be a (relatively) brief period in its history. Ironically, the extreme star formation is causing plumes of material to be ejected above and below the disk of the galaxy - something that will disrupt future stellar birth.\n\nMany of the features of this galaxy were blocked dust and gas at visible wavelengths of light. Webb is capturing never-before-seen detail, including the galaxy\u2019s uniquely distorted disk, likely a result of the galaxy merger - and a staggering number of stars. These 16.5 million are likely only a small portion of the total number of stars that might reside in a galaxy like M82. Hubble data, in yellow, show tendrils of material, which represent ionized hydrogen gas, close to the galaxy\u2019s disk. \n\nGalaxies are intricate ecosystems, and telescopes working together are star and its inclination.\n3- the celestial bodies, stars, planet regularly come to the side of the black hole to take charge of destiny, time and memory to set off again to make their journeys in the PREDICTION OF MY THEORY ON THE FIFTH DIMENSION.\nA VERY IMPORTANT REFLECTION.\nEach galaxy has its own black hole in the center, and each black hole has two faces, one which gives life to the stars, planet, star, nebula etc....,.\n1- a world that dies gives birth to another world, nothing is lost, nothing is created, everything is transformed.\n2- black holes dictate the rotation speed of each star and its inclination.\n3- the celestial bodies, stars, planet regularly come to the side of the black hole to take charge of destiny, time and memory to set off again to make their journeys in the universe.\n4- just at the exit of black hole physically speaking it is the moment t=0, the beginning of all life.\n5- at the exit of the black hole time runs in two directions, a forward direction and another return direction.\n6- we can travel in a black hole but in one direction not in both directions.\n7- a place in the universe devoid of a black hole will see accumulated clusters of galaxies A space volcano? 
These two interacting galaxies almost look like an erupting volcano. Called MCG+12-02-001, this pair is visibly affected by their gravitational interaction, as cosmic material flings out in opposite directions.
Located about 200 million light-years away, these galaxies call the constellation Cassiopeia home: https://go.nasa.gov/3QsN5K6
Image credit: NASA, ESA, the Hubble Heritage (STScI/AURA)-ESA/Hubble Collaboration, and A. Evans (University of Virginia, Charlottesville/NRAO/Stony Brook University)

A space volcano? 
These two interacting galaxies almost look like an erupting volcano. Called MCG+12-02-001, this pair is visibly affected by their gravitational interaction, as cosmic material flings out in opposite directions.
Located about 200 million light-years away, these galaxies call the constellation Cassiopeia home: https://go.nasa.gov/3QsN5K6
Image credit: NASA, ESA, the Hubble Heritage (STScI/AURA)-ESA/Hubble Collaboration, and A. Evans (University of Virginia, Charlottesville/NRAO/Stony Brook University)
